summary:
The stone fortification wall at [[event:2|Jericho]], associated with the [[event:1391|earliest known enclosure walls]] of the settlement, represents one of the earliest known large-scale attempts to enclose and protect a human settlement with a durable stone barrier. The wall at Jericho, dating to approximately 8800–8300 BCE during the Pre-Pottery Neolithic A period, was built from fieldstone and mud mortar, stood several meters high, and was associated with the prominent [[event:1367|round stone tower]] that still stands to a height of over eight meters.

The question of whether the Jericho stone wall was primarily defensive (protecting against human raiders), functional (protecting against seasonal flooding and mud slides), or symbolic and social (marking the community's territory and investment in permanence) remains debated. Its construction required a mobilization of labor and community coordination on a scale unprecedented in the Neolithic world at the time, suggesting that social mechanisms for organizing collective effort were already well developed at Jericho. Whether or not it was built in response to actual external threats, the Jericho fortification wall stands as an enduring symbol of the emergence of the fortified, bounded community — a social form that would define urban life from the early Bronze Age to the modern era.
